OK, so you have a unicode string inside of a regular/byte string which is then interpreted as the raw UTF-8 bytes instead of the Unicode hex points that those bytes represent. That's definitely a bug but it strikes me as one which may be difficult to fix. I am a little wary of messing with the decoding/encoding logic too much because it has gone through a handful of iterations already (I think).

Why isn't it already a unicode string if that is its encoding?  
If you receive it that way (e.g. user input of some sort), have you tried running `s.decode('utf-8')` before feeding it into TOML?  
Is this an issue in Python3 too or only in Python2?

Hi Johannes,

1) That error results where there is more than one genome with the same name as an input file. Could that be the case? If not, would you mind uploading your log file? (located in `log/logger.log`)

2) The problem with small bins is that `mash` ANI estimates are inaccurate for them. If you're genome set is small enough / if you have the computational power, you can run it with `--SkipMash` in which case the small bins should be handled fine.

Best,
-Matt

This is something I've wanted to do for a while, but I'm not sure how to best implement it. For now, I have #125 (which is what I personally use actually) that supports this feature via a meta-backend called `multi`. A config that does nothing when the terminal is focused, uses desktop notifications when unlocked&unfocused, and pushover when locked looks like this:
```yaml
backends: [multi]
multi:
    locked:
        pushover:
            user_key: user-api-key
    unfocused:
        default: {}
    focused: {}
```

I'd prefer it be more integrated rather than a meta-backend but can't decide on the best implementation approach (both code & config).

You can try it out with `pip install git+https://github.com/dschep/ntfy.git@locked#egg=ntfy`

You are right, `kwargs` is a tricky thing in Python.

There are some part of my code need to be refactored about `kwargs` and `args`.

Because, for any `function call` of `object`, while it called by `args`, the first arguments will be `object` itself, later the arguments. On the other side, while it called by `kwargs`, the first arguments will be called argument.

For example:

```python
class Obj(object):
   def func(a=0, b=0, c=0):
       pass
o = Obj()
o.func(1,2,3)
# args is (Obj, 1, 2, 3)
o.func(a=1, b=2, c=3)
# args is (Obj), kwargs is {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
```

But, the above might meet some exception in some edge cases based on [current implementation](https://github.com/note35/sinon/blob/master/sinon/lib/util/Wrapper.py#L97)

I think there is a way to split `args` into 2 part, one for `module/class`, the other for `pure args`.

Also, this needs big efforts too, it impacts several places.

About the algorithm you described:

- I found cases where both module and non-module libraries are present in the same file... Normal fortran compiler takes it... Not sure whether FoBiS.py should account for such cases (maybe not, keep it simple for well-programmed code...), but currently normal compilation works fine because when the module is compiled, these other libraries are as well, even though the dependency tree is broken (surprisingly the same is not true with makefile)
- How can the makefile be bugged and FoBiS.py work properly, if FoBiS.py does rely on "make" ?

The only place where I could see an additional complexity caused by supporting C++, is indeed compilation options. I guess the options overlap to a certain extent, but are not equivalent. So a new aspect would be to provide, like CMake does, different build types such as _debug_ or _release_ and with it associate a set of options for each compiler.

Ok, when thinking in detail about the user-interface though, it **does** become more complex, because you need to provide certain command-line parameter twice. E.g. `--cflags` and `--lflags` would need to be rename to distinguish between fortran and compilers (`-fc` is fine, would just need to be supplemented by `-cc`). User interface *is* an important aspect that determines whether a tool becomes widely used or not (otherwise you guys would have been using CMake and @szaghi would never have started working on FoBiS.py). 

If UI and back-compatibility becomes an issue, then maybe the wisest step in that direction would be to start a new project that branches from FoBiS.py, that would be more general (designed for fortran and C/C++ from the start, maybe another name) and keep FoBiS.py as it is for now. A question then would be, how to make such a parallel development constructive (bug fixes in one portable to the other project). This is probably the point where I would close this issue, and forget about it for now...

@Eljee, I agree with you that using a space as a separator is not nice. It's my other big annoyance about the way symbols are defined in sympy. (Apart from the violation of DRY)

After talking to a mutual friend of ours I decided that parameter creation shouldn't be done with optional extra's. Instead, if other default guesses or ranges are required, you can always set them separately:

```python
a, b = parameters('a, b')
a.value = 2.0
a.min, a.max = 0.0, 10.0
```

I got a bit carried away earlier ;).

I still don't like the violation of DRY though... But it is the least surprising to users, which is important.

Just so you know, I just stumbled over this (I guess somewhat experimental) pure-python dbus client implementation that claims to have support for blocking, asyncio & Tornado I/O<sup>[1]</sup> by utilizing an I/O-free core<sup>[2]</sup>:
https://gitlab.com/takluyver/jeepney

It also has an *high-level interface* that is slightly more low-level that what `txdbus` offers<sup>[1]</sup>:

```py
import asyncio

from jeepney import DBusAddress, new_method_call
from jeepney.integrate.asyncio import connect_and_authenticate

notifications = DBusAddress('/org/freedesktop/Notifications',
                            bus_name='org.freedesktop.Notifications',
                            interface='org.freedesktop.Notifications')

async def send_notification():
    (transport, protocol) = await connect_and_authenticate(bus='SESSION')

    msg = new_method_call(notifications, 'Notify', 'susssasa{sv}i',
                            ('jeepney_test',  # App name
                             0,     # Not replacing any previous notification
                             '',    # Icon
                             'Hello, world!',  # Summary
                             'This is an example notification from Jeepney',
                             [], {},  # Actions, hints
                             -1,      # expire_timeout (-1 = default)
                            ))
    # Send the message and await the reply
    reply = await protocol.send_message(msg)
    print('Notification ID:', reply[0])

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(send_notification())
```

Building something like [`pydbus`'s API](https://github.com/LEW21/pydbus/blob/master/doc/tutorial.rst) on top this could indeed be a viable alternative?

  [1]: https://jeepney.readthedocs.io/en/latest/integrate.html
  [2]: https://jeepney.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

> I don't really understand why you use all those different modules and programming techniques, like caches or why you need to create a client to do a http request for you.

Caches are useful when certain cases that only few entities are frequently queried in a short period of time.  It's anyway turned off by default and the way to store them can be customized (and testable) through the `CachePolicy` interface.

`Client` also has several roles.  First of all, it improves its testability — you don't need any mocks or monkey patches for unit testing.  It also makes you able to customize the policy for HTTP networking without any global states.  For example, you can configure HTTP proxies and timeout settings for networking to Wikidata and at a time also remain HTTP settings to be default in the global level.

之所以会出现CursorNotReadAllDataError这个错误，是因为在python2中，with不支持异步语法，退出with的时候并不能等待连接完成所有数据读取，如果sql查询了超过1条数据，但你只读取第一条，此时退出cursor并继续进行下一次查询，那么就极有可能这些未读取的数据会出现在下一次的结果当中，导致数据混乱，所以with语法退出的时候会检查是否已经完全读取了返回数据，否则就会raise出CursorNotReadAllDataError这个异常，cursor使用fetchall读取所有数据之后就不会再出现这个问题了

Python3支持异步with语法，所以可以安全的使用异步语法保证连接完成数据读取过程

Sorry for delaying on this one, I've been busy. As a recap: 
* the problem (breakage of compatibility with WinXP) was introduced in #761 
* because the exe/wheel files are produced with a more recent Windows version (>= Vista) where `GetTickCount64` is available, also the resulting exes/wheels are compiled with such an assumption in place, hence Win XP is unable to use those public exe/wheels
* Win XP is still compatible if psutil is compiled / installed from source, meaning this issue is with the distribution of exe/wheel files only
* 3.4.2 is the last psutil version where the distributed exe/wheel files were compatible

Ok. Let me explain. The delegate is constantly trying to pull messages from a queue. When the queue is empty, it blocks. This happens inside a listener's `wait()` method. Each delegate has a `listener` object, so it basically loops over its `listener.wait()` calls, roughly equivalent to below:

```python
try:
    while 1:
        msg = j.listener.wait()
        j.on_message(msg)
except Exception as e:
    j.on_close(e)
```

The way I designed it, one natural way to exit the loop is to let it timeout, at which time `listener.wait()` would raise a `WaitTooLong` exception. Another way to exit the loop is to raise some exception inside the `on_message()` handler (e.g. calling `close()` would raise a `StopListening` exception, thus exiting the loop). 

So, my answer to your question is: **there is no simple way to stop all delegate threads in a top-down manner.** That being said, I am also wondering why this ability is important to you. Do you want to ensure the threads do some kind of cleanup as the program terminates?

Another thing I want to point out is, your `timeout` of 900 seconds seems too long. The purpose of that timeout is to stop the thread after a certain period of user inactivity. I know your case is just a test. In a real-life situation, you probably don't want to wait 900 seconds to conclude that a user has left. To me, 60 seconds is more than enough (in nowadays' short attention spans).

Unless you can convince me of this issue's importance, I don't feel motivated to fixing it.

I think ~A is typically the last section in LAS 2.0. However, it is possible that some of the LAS 3.0 sections that are data tables similar to ~A could follow.

As far as I remember the CWLS 2.0 standard, the ~V section is required to be first. After that, the ~W, ~P, ~C, and ~O sections are defined, but not necessarily ordered. I think only ~W and ~C are required. You have to have the STRT, STOP, and STEP parameters and the curve descriptions.  The others can be missing or empty. LAS 3.0 basically introduced optional sections for recording other types of downhole data. They kind of expand the specification of the old free form ~O section.

        attrs=list(dict.values())

        result={}
        for i in range(0,len(strobjs)):

            #print(strobjs[i])

    def readobjs(self,objs):
        """
        read specified objects from imported idf file and store in list
        :param objs:specify objects user want to extract
        :return:
        """
        idf=self.idf
        objlist=[]
        for obj in objs:
            new=idf.idfobjects[obj.upper()]
            objlist.append(new)

        self.objects=objlist

    def readattrs(self,objid,attrs):
        objs=self.objects[objid]
        objList=[]
        for obj in objs:
            attrList=[]
            for attr in attrs:
                #print(obj,attr)
                attrList.append(obj[attr])

            objList.append(attrList)

        if dict==True:
            pass
        else:

Also, I think it'd be clearer to let `switch` and `flat` have separate implementations:

```python
async def switch(source):
    async with AsyncExitStack() as stack:
        # Initialize
        streamer = await stack.enter_context(streamcontext(source))
        streamer_task = asyncio.ensure_future(anext(streamer))
        substreamer_task = None
        # Loop over events
        while streamer_task or substreamer_task:
            wait_tasks = filter(None, (streamer_task, substreamer_task))
            done, _ = await asyncio.wait(wait_tasks, return_when="FIRST_COMPLETED")
            # Substreamer event
            if substreamer_task in done:
                try:
                    yield substreamer_task.result()
                except StopAsyncIteration:
                    await substreamer.aclose()
                    substreamer_task = None
            # Streamer event
            if streamer_task in done:
                try:
                    subsource = streamer_task.result()
                except StopAsyncIteration:
                    streamer_task = None
                else:
                    # Clean up
                    await substreamer.aclose()
                    if substreamer_task:
                        substreamer_task.cancel()
                    # New substream
                    streamer_task = asyncio.ensure_future(anext(streamer))
                    substreamer = await stack.enter_context(streamcontext(subsource))
                    substreamer_task = asyncio.ensure_future(anext(substreamer)
```

So with manhole would be having something like this:

```python
import os
import sys
import time
import manhole
manhole.install(oneshot_on='USR2', connection_handler=manhole.handle_connection_exec)

def do_stuff():
    time.sleep(1)
    print(time.time())

print('PID:', os.getpid())
while True:
    do_stuff()
```

Then, to enable tracing: `echo 'import hunter; hunter.trace()' | scripts/manhole-cli -2 <PID>`
And to stop: `echo 'import hunter; hunter.stop()' | scripts/manhole-cli -2 <PID>`

You should `pip install https://github.com/ionelmc/python-manhole/archive/0de63f717350fbb59a0573f56c7c77ae085037bb.zip` first tho, as the `connection_handler` option ain't released yet.

Let me know how it works.

PS. You can do it without manhole, in a secure fashion. You just need to open a unix domain socket, check if connecting user is the right one (SO_PEERCRED), and read the start/stop commands from the socket in your signal handler. For the client part you can use socat/netcat.

Yeah, it turns out color technology is a nightmare when it comes to correctness, because it's all about big tables of numbers and complicated formulas that you have to copy out of papers, *and* there aren't any solid "gold standard" values to check your computations against. And if you do get something wrong then maybe it looks a little weird, but who will notice, really? You can test against other implementations, but sometimes they don't exist, or they can be wrong too... I did a lot of work tracking down reference values for the colorspacious test suite, but even that mostly checks that computations are correct for given inputs; it doesn't tell you that the inputs are correct, and these constants are inputs :-/.

That sounds like you have an older pyOpenSSL, and the newer Twisted requires a newer one. I don't think it's magic-wormhole specific.

Could you try running just `python -c "from twisted.internet import reactor"` on that box, in both the twisted-17.1.0 case and the downgraded case? If everything is happy, that command will exit without printing anything, but if it's unhappy, then it should fail with that same "AttributeError" traceback you saw from invoking magic-wormhole. If that's the case, then at least we've removed magic-wormhole from the potential home of the problem.

It looks like twisted-17.1.0 was the first release to have a hard requirement that pyOpenSSL provides `SSL.OP_NO_TLSv1_1`. Twisted-16.6.0 and earlier used `getattr(SSL, "OP_NO_TLSv1_1", 0)` and could survive if pyOpenSSL didn't have it. Twisted correctly declares its dependency on the newer pyOpenSSL, but maybe some version of `pip` isn't respecting that declaration.

Try this:

```
% python
> import OpenSSL
> print OpenSSL
> print OpenSSL.__version__
> [name for name in dir(OpenSSL.SSL) if name.startswith("OP_NO_")]
```

that should tell you where pyOpenSSL is being imported from, what version it is, and a full list of all the `OP_NO_` symbols it provides. That might give us a clue as to what's broken about it. My hunch is that you've got something ancient like pyOpenSSL-0.13.

Installing magic-wormhole directly into `/usr/` with `sudo pip` means you're either using or replacing the other python libraries up there too. I don't know whether pip will see the dependency and upgrade your system pyOpenSSL for you, or if it will ignore it (and then you get runtime errors like this).

In general, my recommendation is to install magic-wormhole (and other python applications) into a virtualenv, where pip can install everything you need without affecting the rest of your system. I haven't found a nice way to do this for multiple users though (e.g. installing it once in /usr/local vs installing it with `pipsi` into `~/.local/` for every user on that box). You might check out Glyph's presentation at the last PyCon on the subject: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5BqAeN-F9Qs .

If you can't do that, then my recommendation is to *upgrade* the dependencies, rather than *downgrade* Twisted. I haven't yet looked at everything magic-wormhole needs from Twisted to determine what the oldest version we can possibly use is, but my usual assumption (not necessarily a good one) is it's at least whatever version of Twisted was present when I made the release. So running against a significantly older Twisted might run into other problems. If you discover those, let me know and I'll make magic-wormhole correctly declare a dependency upon the newer one. But the result will be the same: more demands from pesky software asking you to upgrade everything :).

__data class Foo(int x, int y) { ... }

    labels: List[str] = field(factory=list)

@GabrielRF How do you start your bots? Via `nohup`? 
I'm on VDS now and starting bots from non-root leads to their stopping.

To find out whether it's Telegram problem or it's bug in your code, could you start your bots in separate virtual terminals? (using `screen` utility):
1. `sudo apt-get install screen` (in Ubuntu)
2. `screen -S mybot` (create virtual terminal with 555.mybot ID, where 555 is given Process ID (autogenerated))
3. `./bot.py` or `python3.4 bot.py` (starts the bot)
4. Ctrl+A, then Ctrl+D (detaches from that terminal, you should see previous command prompt, hovewer, your bot is still working in foreground)

When you stop recieving messages from your bot, do the following:
1. `screen -list` will show you created virtual terminals.
2. Find the one that has `mybot` in its name (f.ex. `32200.mybot`)
3. Type `screen -r 32200.mybot` to switch to that terminal.
There you should see any exceptions or errors occured and find out the reason.

P.S. Of course, one can just do `nohup ./mybot.py &` and then look through `nohup.out` file for errors, but `screen` variant somehow works better for me (placebo?)

I think the issue comes from using `BaseTranslationFormSet`. This one is intended for making a formset of all translations of a single object. Here, it would be used to edit a single answer in all available languages.

What you seem to want here, is a regular formset, of translatable forms. That is, a formset in only one language, displaying language-aware forms. For displaying a formset of several answers, all of them in one language, you should stick with a regular inline formset, and make it work on a `TranslatableModelForm`. 

I am no expert on the admin submodule, but I believe here it should actually work out of the box if your custom formset inherits Django's `BaseInlineFormSet`? Have you tried it? How does it work (or not work)?

There you go. It made things somewhat more complicated though, because of `X100`:

> If an error to be ignored, no longer occurs for a given file, this will cause an `X100` error, in order to make you progressively reduce the number of ignores as legacy code gets rewritten or removed.

If we would just expand the filename with `glob.glob()`, and treat each resulting file individually, you would get an `X100` for each ignored error that doesn't occur in **all** files matching the glob pattern. But instead we only want an `X100` if an ignored error doesn't occur in **any** file matching the glob pattern.

Therefore, we have to process all files that match the same pattern in one step. This also means that we can no longer just patch the function that is called for each file, but need to hijack a more high-level API. However, as  a side effect, it now also works with flake8 3.0.0 (previously we required >=3.2.1).

> We changed the state dir to harden against a possible symlink attack (the state file, socket and log file could be redirected to some other location via a symlink to trick watchman into writing some place else).

Interesting - thanks for the explanation. IIUC, you're saying that, in the case where you're running `watchman` as a daemon, an attacker could set up a symlink in order to cause the daemon to clobber important files.

I suppose that's only relevant if you're using `watchman` as a daemon; what exactly is the rationale for doing so? Having a smaller footprint by having one process, rather than a process per user with possibly overlapping watches/indexes/etc (along with the requisite kernel resources)?

On NixOS, while packages are required to be immutable, *services* may have state. Thus, in NixOS, I think we could provide a binary that uses `/tmp` by default, but the service would use the `--sockname`/`--logfile`/`--statefile` flags to effectively make the "statedir" be `/var/lib/watchman`, with the sticky bit set. Enabled services can also add to the system's shell profile, so it would be trivial for us to set up a `watchman` alias that passes the right flags.

Ultimately, I don't want us to have to choose between a binary that doesn't work without the service running, or a binary that works without but is susceptible to attack when run as `root`.

I'm building debian packages for libzmq and pyzmq. For this I (so far) always build a new libzmq and pyzmq package so I expect them to always match APIs. Especially autogenerated constants in pyzmq. I've changed it to enable drafts now so I get a consistent pair of debs.

For me I think pyzmq should match the settings libzmq has on compile. But I can see you concerns that the next libzmq version could break pyzmq without recompile. I recompile so that isn't an issue for me.

So I think pyzmq should by default half enable drafts. Thinks like the event constants for drafts would be save to enable. If they later change this would only break applications using the drafts api, which is expected but would not effect anyone else. Especially constants that stable functions like the monitoring socket will emit to unsuspecting downstream packages.

How did you install libzmq? I believe libzmq enables drafts by default, but it may depend on context.

I would like to enable drafts by default in pyzmq to match libzmq, but since they are draft APIs, libzmq has promised to make breaking changes to them even in patch releases until they are promoted out of draft status. That makes the calculus different for whether pyzmq should support drafts by default from libzmq itself. libzmq supporting drafts by default means that downstream packages using the draft API should expect to be broken by any patch release of libzmq. I don't think that's an appropriate default behavior for pyzmq. But if pyzmq doesn't link the draft APIs

So your current choices are:

1. log/ignore unhandled event types (e.g. new events not yet known to pyzmq, or to you if you've included your own definitions for event constants)
2. build pyzmq with `--enable-drafts`
3. build libzmq with `--disable-drafts`

In general, I think 1. is the best choice, because it ensures your code is safe for a given pyzmq version even after libzmq is upgraded to promote constants out of draft status, or introduce new events. Neither of the other two make such a guarantee, as new releases of libzmq can always define new events and constants that pre-existing pyzmq releases can't know about.

I do not think we should have the same behavior for ``set_exception``. For the following reasons:

1) ``'cancelled'`` and ``'failed'`` are essentially the same state as they both will throw an exception on ``future.result()`` and run any cleanup methods because of it. The only difference between failed and cancelled is that it gives you a little more grainularity on if it was the user that explicitly caused the task to fail or not. Thus knowing the difference between 'cancelled' and 'failed' is not as important as the difference between 'cancelled' and 'success'
2) Note that the ``set_exception`` can be ran at any time for any task. So it may be misleading on what caused the task to not be successful. If ``set_exception`` can override cancels and a cancel initiates the shutdown of a task, that would propogate the exception instead of the cancelled exception (even though the cancel happened first). Ideally, when the state of the future is not success it should always raise what was the incident to kick it off and by not allowing ``set_exception`` to override ``cancel``, it is better able to do that.

No matter in the end, I do not think we can perfectly determine if a cancel error or another error should be the one propogated if they nearly happened at the same time, but in the end, it is not really a big issue because failed and cancelled state have essentially the same behavior and workflow.

Normally we try to automatically provide the right imports for annotations too, so `monkeytype apply` doesn't result in modules with missing imports. However, I don't think that's possible with the current runtime implementation of `NewType`; the calling module is not stored anywhere in the resulting function object, so there's no way we can introspect it. I hear you @dangra !  My reply is just for completeness -- I would respect any decision you take... hopefully at least you would ship tests/ within source distribution.

Additional dependencies are indeed a hassle, and different projects deal with it in various ways, most common approach just skip specific tests (or entire test module) if necessary dependency is absent (e.g. in PyMVPA we can run tests reliably without any external dependency but numpy although we have probably around 10 optional dependencies).  Some (e.g. psychopy) also include complete py.test runner along to simplify running on some exotic systems without py.test being readily available.

As for comparison to other projects -- don't forget that we are quite special, since we are using Python, which come with builtin unittests module.  Relatively small feature but imho it is the one fostered culture of better testing (on average ;-) ) among its projects.  So, to my experience, majority of Python projects (numpy, scipy, pandas, sklearn, PyMVPA, even fail2ban now ;-), ...long list) ship with tests either under module/tests or even spread out per each submodule.  Many of them even provide "module.test()" call-in to trigger testing.
Complementary to this,  relying on tests being provided, such initiatives as DEP8 in the Debian world (http://dep.debian.net/deps/dep8) now blossoms with http://ci.debian.net/ which allows to identify causes for breakage while considering entire OS integration.
Hopefully this convinces you that it is actually quite practical.  I agree though that it does require a tiny bit more of work.

This seems really useful and I really like the idea. However, I am reluctant to merge this just yet for two reasons:

1. I'm not sure if I like `mode='a'`, as opposed to something like `append=True`. Mode to me is something you specify when you open a file handler, but calling `dd.io.save` doesn't "open" a file handler, since `save` opens and closes it (which is essentially an implementation detail that the user of `save` should not even be exposed to). It also exposes too much of the internals - let's say `save` gets another backend one day, in which `mode` is not used. Then we would have to start parsing the `mode` and replicate the semantics. Which leads me to...
 
2. The semantics are not what you would expect. You can currently only append or update root groups. If I want to update or add to a subgroup, I get an error message. I think I'd like to hold off on introducing an append feature until you can append and update a group of any level.

I will close this for now, but it's definitely a very useful feature that I would like to see in deepdish some day.

Instead of copying the sh import error catching everywhere, I think it would be methodologically better to put it in one place (a new p4a file) and then every other file can import it from there.

That said, I don't think there's much point in doing this unless p4a is changed to actually work on windows, doing something like https://github.com/kivy/python-for-android/pull/994 (filling in all the gaps, polishing it, and actually supporting it post-merge). Barring that, I think it would be better to detect the windows environment and exit with an error message before even trying the imports.

Thanks for the PR, but for these reasons I won't merge these changes.

Btw, there is also the `FreeType` font engine, which is used on linux, and can (apparently) be used for a windows build. 
If one gets a build of qt where directwrite (or freetype) is enabled, it will support setting up hinting with `QFont.setHintingPreference`, and we will be able to choose what we prefer. 


 | PreferDefaultHinting  | PreferNoHinting | PreferVerticalHinting | PreferFullHinting
---------------------- | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- | -------------
Win Vista (w/o Platform Update) and earlier |	Full hinting | Full hinting | Full hinting | Full hinting
Win 7 and Win Vista (w/Platform Update) and DirectWrite enabled in Qt | Full hinting | Vertical hinting | Vertical hinting | Full hinting
FreeType	| Operating System setting | No hinting | Vertical hinting (light) | Full hinting
Cocoa on Mac OS X | No hinting | No hinting | No hinting | No hinting

Thanks for starting this.

**Travis**
The immediate problem is that you've added a file named fire.py, so the test that tries to "import fire" ends up importing your file as the fire module, rather than the actual fire module. Using a different file name will get around this issue.

**Development workflow**
Like you say, there's other work to do before merging this to master (tests, documentation, etc). I'd be open to creating a feature branch for this if you prefer to work on this over multiple separate pull requests.

**Code review**
From a quick glance at the script, one issue I notice is that we shouldn't be modifying sys.argv. Instead, we'll want to modify the `Fire` function to accept a list for argv (currently it only accepts a string for the argument 'command', not a list).

Re-opening this issue.

As mentioned in the first comment, simulating the effect of detector effects like IPC requires generation of individual low-resolution exposures. The `interleaveImages` routine combines a dither sequence (obtained in some specific way, suitable only for simulations) of multiple exposures to obtain a well resolved image. In GalSim, it is much faster to draw a high resolution image of a profile than to draw multiple low-resolution exposures, even if the number of sampling points is the same. Therefore, it is only natural to have an `deInterleaveImages` routine that does the opposite of `interleaveImages` routine, i.e., given a high resolution image, it generates a dither sequence by slicing the image. I am re-opening this issue to address this aspect.

The [CMake coding standards](http://docs.ros.org/api/catkin/html/user_guide/standards.html) for catkin state that closing keywords like `endif()` and `endforeach()` should not repeat the condition.

In other words, it's a matter of policy (just like indentation) and happens to be decided for ROS in this particular way. If it bothers you, you can either use the `--ignore endblock_args` command line option or add a comment saying `#catkin_lint: ignore endblock_args` near the beginning (before the first `if()`) of your `CMakeLists.txt`.

> Installing python and python's package is possible in user space, no root access needed. But could be painful or too much for non python user (user doesn't mean developer here), but note that it could be automated in some way, but could require some privileges.

I concur. In fact, this is why I created a pull request to add `docopts` to Homebrew/Linuxbrew (see https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-devel-only/pull/60). In this way you should be able to do simply:
```
$ brew install docopts
```
as your (non-privileged) user and then have it installed within (a subfolder of) your home directory, so that you can use it as usual.

Under the hood what `brew` does is creating a virtualenv (see the formula: [docopts.rb#L28-L31](https://github.com/CristianCantoro/homebrew-devel-only/blob/86f5a48a86204a7acb15391a9ba3a248394bbf70/Formula/docopts.rb#L28-L29)) where `docopt` v.0.6.1 is installed. Homebrew, then takes care of copying the binary in a place that already part of your `PATH` ([docopts.rb#L33](https://github.com/CristianCantoro/homebrew-devel-only/blob/86f5a48a86204a7acb15391a9ba3a248394bbf70/Formula/docopts.rb#L33)) and do some testing ([docopts.rb#L36-L45](https://github.com/CristianCantoro/homebrew-devel-only/blob/86f5a48a86204a7acb15391a9ba3a248394bbf70/Formula/docopts.rb#L36-L45)).

As of now I have two issues;
* without a tagged revision I can not have a PR merged in core ([Homebrew/homebrew-core](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/)), but in the devel-only repo which is currently deprecated (and my PR has been rejected (https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-devel-only/pull/60)), hence https://github.com/docopt/docopts/issues/10.
* from the CI results, several test fail for `docopts` fail on Mac (alas, I can not recover the link with the results). On Linux I still have test no. 104 failing (https://github.com/docopt/docopts/issues/6).

I hope I am not too much off-topic.

I had a similar issue: same ImportError, though the corresponding warning was different:

```
g++ -bundle -undefined dynamic_lookup -L/Users/gmulc/anaconda/lib -arch x86_64 -arch x86_64 build/temp.macosx-10.6-x86_64-2.7/dynet.o -L. -L/Users/gmulc/dynet-base/dynet/build/dynet/ -L/Users/gmulc/anaconda/lib -L/Users/gmulc/dynet-base/dynet/build/dynet/ -ldynet -o /Users/gmulc/dynet-base/dynet/build/python/_dynet.so
clang: warning: libstdc++ is deprecated; move to libc++ with a minimum deployment target of OS X 10.9
ld: warning: object file (build/temp.macosx-10.6-x86_64-2.7/dynet.o) was built for newer OSX version (10.7) than being linked (10.6)
```

This seems to be because `_dynet.so` looks for `libdynet.dylib` at `@rpath/libdynet.dylib` which doesn't work unless `/path/to/dynet/build/dynet` is in `@rpath`. You can update it with `install_name_tool -change @rpath/libdynet.dylib /path/to/dynet/build/dynet/libdynet.dylib _dynet.so` (and then verify the change with `otool -L _dynet.so`).

Not sure why the shared library path is like that in the first place, but this could at least be a workaround. Hope it works for you!

My vote is to not use the NWS velocity colormap as a default. The green red is very colorblind unfriendly.  A diverging blue red(BuRd18 maybe) would be better or blue yellow.   
  
Differential reflectivity should go a little bit into the negative side. Maybe make vmin -1. It makes it easier to find differential attenuation, and negative Zdrs are valid for higher frequencies, and for ice in some conditions at lower frequencies.    

Although not in scope of this PR, ideally differential phase would be 0 to 360 or -180 to 180(or 0 to 180) depending on how differential phase is arranged in the file. A quick max/min check could achieve this. 

Rain rate could probably have a much lower maximum by default, maybe 50? 

Probably also outside the realm of this PR, but it would be nice to have some variables have a default norm applied(rhohv, NCP) that compress the low end of the range(0-.9 for rhohv for instance) and then expand out the upper side of the range.

Release notes:

----


This release contains three large API additions or changes:

-   Official support of the [Google Cloud Bigtable][1] API
-   Official support of the [Google Cloud Logging][2] API
-   Upgrading [Google Cloud Datastore][3] from `v1beta2` to `v1beta3`

## BigQuery

-   Add a `template_suffix` parameter to BigQuery `Table.insert_data` (#1562).
-   Expand/clarify synchronous query usage docs (#1674).
-   Cache job constructed for a synchronous query (#1675).

## Storage

-   Pass mutated properties through to `Bucket.create` API call (#1579).
-   Allow predefined ACL names from either XML or JSON APIs (#1664).

## Pub/Sub

-   Add `Topic.list_subscriptions` (#1580).
-   Fix topic subscription path patterns (#1592).
-   Add `Message.service_timestamp` property (#1627).
-   Add support for IAM permissions on topics (#1640, #1641, #1642, #1652)
  and subscriptions (#1644, #1645, #1646)

## Datastore

-   Upgrading from `v1beta2` to `v1beta3` (see #1288). In particular
    -   the `Query.group_by` property was renamed to `Query.distinct_on`
    -   explicit support for null values in entities was added (#1464)
    -   explicit support for Geo Point values in entities was added (#1464)
    -   removing custom `GCLOUD_DATASET_ID` environment variable and
        just relying on `GCLOUD_PROJECT` (#1465)
-   Fix broken docs for `Query.add_filter` (#1622).
-   Documentation: Filtering Query by key is also possible (#1632).
-   Allow bytes or string as project value in datastore clients (#1638).

## DNS

-   Renaming `type->record_type` in DNS doc (#1663).

[1]: https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/
[2]: https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/
[3]: https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/concepts/overview

```python
from gcloud.credentials import get_for_service_account_json
from gcloud import datastore

# Working on making this part easier / shorter
creds1 = get_for_service_account_json('path/to/key1.json').create_scoped(

    datastore.SCOPE)
conn2 = datastore.Connection(credentials=creds2)

bucket1 = storage.get_bucket('bucketname1', connection=conn1)
bucket2 = storage.get_bucket('bucketname2', connection=conn2)

bucket1.delete_blob('blob-name1.txt')
bucket2.delete_blob('blob-name2.txt')
```

One solution that we've come up with (credit to my colleague JR) is to add a new field to the package sources & stack definitions to indicate when/where template loading should be done relative to that package, e.g.:
 ```
package_sources:
  git:
   - uri: ssh://git@bitbucket..com/myorg/stacker-modules.git
     template_package_name: mystackermodules

stacks:
  mystack:
    template_package_name: mystackermodules
    template_path: cloudformation/ssm_parameter.yaml
```
In this case, because stack `mystack` has a defined template_package_name, its template loading will be done relative to that downloaded package's directory.

I believe this just comes down to the way the resolver cache in pex works. essentially, once something is resolved the end product of the resolve will be cached for the duration of the resolver cache ttl. the default ttl is `None`, which causes pex to essentially reuse the cache dir forever. it should not be the case that pex prefers sdists over bdists tho.

I think a simple workaround here would be to pass one of either of these flags:

```
    --disable-cache     Disable caching in the pex tool entirely.
    --cache-ttl=CACHE_TTL
                        The cache TTL to use for inexact requirement
                        specifications.
```

however it's certainly a problem area that I'd like to address soon.

Here's an example template that integrates a SAM stack with a custom HTTPS domain using ACM and Route53. It adds `DomainName`, `BasePathMapping` and `RecordSetGroup` resources, hooking up the default `Prod` stage of the `ServerlessRestApi` implicit API, referencing `CertificateArn` (e.g., `arn:aws:acm:us-east-1:[account]:certificate/[id]`, `DomainName` (e.g., `api.example.com`) and `HostedZoneName` (e.g., `example.com.`) as input Parameters.

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: 2010-09-09
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Parameters:
  CertificateArn:
    Type: String
  DomainName:
    Type: String
  HostedZoneName:
    Type: String
    AllowedPattern: '.*\.$'
    ConstraintDescription: Must end with trailing dot `.`
Resources:
  MyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Events:
        API:
          Type: Api
          [...etc...]
  APIDomainName:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::DomainName
    Properties:
      CertificateArn: !Ref CertificateArn
      DomainName: !Ref DomainName
  APIBasePathMapping:
    Type: AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping
    Properties:
      DomainName: !Ref APIDomainName
      RestApiId: !Ref ServerlessRestApi
      Stage: Prod
  APIDomain:
    Type: AWS::Route53::RecordSetGroup
    Properties:
      HostedZoneName: !Ref HostedZoneName
      RecordSets:
      - Name: !Ref DomainName
        Type: A
        AliasTarget:
          DNSName: !GetAtt APIDomainName.DistributionDomainName
          HostedZoneId: Z2FDTNDATAQYW2 # static ID for CloudFront aliases
Outputs:
  ApiUrl:
    Value: !Sub "https://${DomainName}"
```

For future SAM support, for the simple use-case it might be possible to wrap up these resources into a more user-friendly `CustomDomain` object appended to `Serverless::Function` or `Serverless::Api` resources, e.g.:

```yaml
  MyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      CustomDomain: # Creates implicit DomainName, BasePathMapping and RecordSetGroup resources
        CertificateArn: !Ref CertificateArn # Required
        DomainName: !Ref DomainName # Required
        # HostedZoneName not required; can be derived from provided DomainName
```
Of course, this makes lots of simplifying assumptions (the API is mapped to the root path of the subdomain, no other `RestApi`s share the same `DomainName`, the Route53 Hosted Zone and ACM certificate both exist, etc etc..), but I think it could still be interesting for the simple use-case.

Simply wrap your pre-trained estimator object into a `sklearn.PMMLPipeline` object:
```Python
estimator = pickle.load(...)
pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
  ("pretrained-estimator", estimator)
])

sklearn2pmml(pipeline, "pretrained-estimator.pmml")
```

The main problem with using pre-trained estimators is that the resulting PMML document will be devoid of any supporting (meta-)information such as column names or data types. All input fields will be called `x{index}`, and they will be of some numeric data type (typically, `float` for decision tree-based model types, and `double` for all other model types).

If you have some knowledge about the "schema" of your pre-trained models, then it's possible to enhance the `sklearn2pmml.PMMLPipeline` object accordingly:
```Python
dataframemapper = pickle.load(...)
pipeline = PMMLPIpeline([
  ("pretrained-dataframemapper", dataframemapper),
  ("pretrained-estimator", estimator)
])
```
or
```Python
pipeline = PMMLPipeline([
  ("pretrained_estimator", estimator)
])
pipeline.target_field = "Species"
pipeline.active_fields = numpy.array(["Sepal.Length", "Sepal.Width", "Petal.Length", "Petal.Width"])
```

I wonder if this behaviour in MISP (i.e. 'unpublish' after adding new attributes) is intended so that there needs to be a concsious decision to publish the new attributes.

Perhaps it is better to use PyMISP as follows (for example):

```
p = PyMISP(...)
event = p.new_event(info="some published event", published=False)
p.add_filename(event, 'some_file_name')
p.publish(event)
```

This seems to work.

Note there is a bug in PyMISP with the above approach when you set 'published=True' in the call to new_event() and later call p.publish(event) PyMISP thinks the event is already published and doesn't update the event in MISP.

Hi Hongbo,

Thanks for the interest in our code!  I'm not really familiar with the TIE,
so let me say what AMG can do.

Basically, you need to form a CSR matrix in the scipy.sparse.csr_matrix
format, call it ,matrix "A".  Then you pass it into a PyAMG multigrid
solver routine.  This then gives you a solver which will let you solver
A x = b
to within some tolerance.

If the sparse matrix is diffusion-like, then there's a good chance that AMG
will work well.  My recommendation would be to just try PyAMG on your
matrix, and see how it performs.

- Jacob

sorry, realized i didn't answer your question about long term maintenance of the traditional method. The traditional method as it exists right now works by chance,  not by design. I don't see a problem with the traditional method continuing to exist as is, but i don't think its wise to put resources into supporting the traditional method if it fails to meet an individual's needs. In any case when you strip away the plugin related code, the REST endpoints and everything else,  the traditional method boils down to:

```python

app = celery.Celery('girder_worker', 
    backend='amqp://guest@localhost/'',

    'my_custom_package.tasks.my_complex_task', 
    job['args'], job['kwargs'], 
    queue=job.get('celeryQueue'), 
    headers={
             # custom configurations here
    })
```

Hi @adrian62

The grouping of message is done using message type, announcement / withdrawal, AFI / SAFI then next-hop. Changing this structure would cause a major surprise to current users and while I am not opposed to changing it, I try to not make sure that users of the poorly documented API do not suffer more than strictly necessary.

I agree that the next-hop makes little sense in the context of flow routes (but the fact that some draft used to code some information into it - I would need to investigate to remember exactly when it could occur), and that your grouping proposal makes sense too.

If BGP wire encoding was not a total and utter mess, I would have surely represented things following the natural structure of the message. But everything get dumped as attribute or within the NLRI, I resist this idea.

That said even your proposal has some issue. the next-hop is not under the mp-reach-nlri but in the attribute ...

If you want to know what I think of the on-wire BGP encoding and why is it should be replaced, please read http://thomas.mangin.com/data/pdf/LINX%2099%20-%20Mangin%20-%20Good%20Bad%20Missing.pdf and I missed a few things writing the slides ..

> I'd rather have several short, one-purpose decorators than a long, single, multi-purpose one.

I would tend to agree with this, except that these behaviors *already* seem very closely coupled throughout `client.py`. It seems (at least so far), everywhere that `@check_resource` is used, the code in the OP is repeated (or if it isn't, it was intended to be). Just look at how many places I removed those lines from `client.py`.

> if we put it here the condition will also be unnecessarily evaluated in the case of image-oriented functions. Seems like a bad practice even though the overhead is negligible.

Perhaps, but this is already the case. Well before I came along, `@check_resource` already pulled double duty between containers and images. Perhaps it makes more sense to have nearly identical, but separately named decorators for images and containers (e.g., `@check_image` and `@check_container`). I can do that, but that seems to be a more significant change than this was intended to be.

All I really meant to do here was an incremental improvement towards DRY without disturbing the status quo too much. Ultimately, it's your call whether such a thing is worth merging. Either way, the fact that the code in the OP shows up in so many places is probably indicative that *something* needs to be done.

Not related directly with this PR but I have comment regarding if the Publish Service is the best place to have file extension functionality. It looks to me that file extension doesn't concern the Publish Service. It is more of Transmission concern where if the transmission is a file-based one then we worry about the file extension. So to me this function can be a candidate for refactoring to move towards transmission domain.

The current behavior (replacing dots with underscores in the hostname) seems to be there from the initial commit of the `PingCollector` (https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/commit/be5b89d753ea90b9fe07d70d768cd72cc2c92492). The remark in the documentation was added in https://github.com/python-diamond/Diamond/commit/d62087af7e05412ce7d1cc0ac7a36d2e5c4fcebe, but did not match the implementation at that time. So it was either misunderstanding of the implementation, wishful thinking, or some change that was planned but forgotten.

In my opinion, it seems to be a good idea to implement what's described in the documentation, as the part after `target_` in the key is otherwise unused anyway. That would be a BC break however.

You seem to have accidentally stumbled upon a feature that was never supposed to be there. And I am afraid that [I am the cause](https://github.com/conda/conda/commit/123849f484ddb3eb86fc7409a980b1346aa398fd).

In [this line](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/cli/install.py#L140), you see that `conda` is instructed to reject update specs that are anything but bare package names (well, you'd have to look at the definition of `arg2spec` to see for sure). 

So what was supposed to happen when `--all` is present? Well, in [conda 3.x](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/3.x/conda/cli/install.py#L132), it was illegal to supply packages alongside `--all`. This is about the time I began working on conda.

In that first commit above, I revamp the `conda update` functionality, and I inadvertently [removed the check](https://github.com/conda/conda/commit/123849f484ddb3eb86fc7409a980b1346aa398fd#diff-34bb8d01f991a5ffabae5e80b987c83fL130) that ensured that package specs and`--all` do not mix. I can assure you that it was inadvertent.

Honestly, I sort of like the behavior this causes for `--all`, but it makes no sense for it to be there for `--all` and not without it. So we either allow it for all update commands or we remove it here.

Upgrade to Curator 4.2.4. See https://github.com/elastic/curator/issues/832

I understand the pain, but the difficulty here is that Curator will never know what may or may not have happened from any other API call somewhere else.  It's a dangerous assumption that Curator is the only thing that may have caused a change in the cluster state metadata endpoint.  How can Curator guarantee that nothing changed?  By pulling it again and comparing it to the cached version, which kind of invalidates the time and data savings.

I'm very wary of the potential to mess something up in the cluster with this approach, especially since some current actions, and some future planned ones, have the potential to change the cluster state in exactly the ways described (rollover API, reindex API, shrink API, etc).

@ehf5307 my bad I jumped the gun here. 

i noticed you're trying to create an index template by using the search template method. 

There's a sublt difference here. 

1. [Search Template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/search-template.html)

```python
es = Elasticsearch()
resp = es.put_template(id=template_name, body=body)
```

2. [Index Template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-templates.html)

```python
es = Elasticsearch()
resp = es.indices.put_template(name=template_name, body=body)
```

Either way I am afraid this change doesn't fit into the library itself.

RHEL, Fedora and CentOS based distributions:
```
sudo rpm --import https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc
sudo sh -c 'echo -e "[azure-cli]\nname=Azure CLI\nbaseurl=https://packages.microsoft.com/yumrepos/azure-cli\nenabled=1\ngpgcheck=1\ngpgkey=https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc" > /etc/yum.repos.d/azure-cli.repo'
yum check-update
sudo yum install -y azure-cli
```

openSUSE and SLE based distributions:
```
sudo sh -c 'echo -e "[azure-cli]\nname=Azure CLI\nbaseurl=https://packages.microsoft.com/yumrepos/azure-cli\nenabled=1\ntype=rpm-md\ngpgcheck=1\ngpgkey=https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc" > /etc/zypp/repos.d/azure-cli.repo'
sudo zypper refresh
sudo zypper install code
```

This information should appear on a docs.microsoft.com page soon if all is well.

Depending on how migrated repositories are, I'd suggest the following (at least temporary) if you plan to move repositories to use `types`:

## if you still are maintaining `hooks.yaml`

Convert your hooks.yaml to a skeleton providing the minimum possible to satisfy `<0.15.0` and add a `minimum_pre_commit_version: 0.15.0` key to each hook.

For example ([original here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/e9446ff0cc8ffa65309e0a37c247518127a3bf18/hooks.yaml#L1-L7)):

```yaml
-   id: autopep8-wrapper
    language: system
    name: upgrade-your-pre-commit-version
    entry: upgrade-your-pre-commit-version
    files: ''
    minimum_pre_commit_version: 0.15.0
```

- For `pre-commit<0.6.7`, an error about not being able to execute `upgrade-your-pre-commit-version` will be produced
- For `pre-commit>=0.6.7,<0.12.0` an error suggesting an upgrade
- For `pre-commit>=0.12.0`, `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` will be used instead (see below)

## forward-backward compatible `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml`

- Add `minimum_pre_commit_version: 0.15.0` to each hook using `types`
- Change `files: ...` to `files: ''` (this is the default value when `files` is missing)
- The entire reason for the noop `files` is old versions of pre-commit won't even load the configuration far enough to produce a useful error message on `minimum_pre_commit_version`

For example ([original here](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks/blob/e9446ff0cc8ffa65309e0a37c247518127a3bf18/.pre-commit-hooks.yaml#L15-L20)):

```yaml
-   id: check-ast
    name: Check python ast
    description: Simply check whether the files parse as valid python.
    entry: check-ast
    language: python
    files: ''
    types: [python]
    minimum_pre_commit_version: 0.15.0
```

- `pre-commit<0.12.0` won't read this file
- `pre-commit>0.12.0,<=0.15.0` will suggest an upgrade
- `pre-commit>=0.15.0` will use `types` and `files` will be a noop

When you don't care about old versions, see below

## once you no longer care about old versions of pre-commit

- Rremove the noop `files: ''` and the `minimum_pre_commit_version`.

The idea of the `request` was given some parameter that might be used by the developer to "connect" all those signals that are happening for a specific request.

Another way to do it, and maybe more clean, is giving a `context` param at each signal call which will be the same for each unique request. By default, it might be a dictionary but the user could replace the factory, perhaps:

```python
await on_request_start(session, context):
    context.started = loop.time()

await on_request_end(session, context):
    contex.total = loop.time() - session.started

TraceContext = NamedTuple("TraceContext", ['started', 'total'])
tracer = RequestTracing(ctx_factory=TraceContext)
tracer. on_request_start(on_request_start)
tracer. on_request_end(on_request_start)
session = ClientSession(request_tracing=tracer)
```

That's just a sketch, does it sound reasonable for you @asvetlov ?

Just realized that the `TCPConnector` is in fact very coupled to the `ClientSession`, indeed the destructor of the `ClientSession` closes all the opened connections that are held by the `TCPConnector`, without taking care if it was instantiated by the Session or was given as a parameter. Therefore, the rationale that I exposed about the feature of share the `TCPConnetor` and the `DNSResolver` is after all not valid. The context won't be necessary for that case, let's do it explicitly.

Not sure this is the thread to bring this up: With regards to advanced mode. I'm not sure why we have this mode. In my view, if someone is using the command line interface, they're already "advanced" - If we're looking to not confuse people by showing them too many commands they may not need to know about, we could make it so that running `blockstack` or `blockstack --help` shows only the "basic" commands and that running something like `blockstack --help-advanced` shows info about the other commands. That there's state around which arguments are accepted on the command line makes it confusing to help people. I can't just say run `blockstack get_name_blockchain_record larry.id` - I need to first ask them to make sure they have advanced mode enabled.

Hi @carlos-jenkins,

First of all thanks for the detailed response. We appreciate both your efforts on this PR as well as your patience on following up in the discussion! :+1:

> This will also imply that all users must add the @pytest.mark.xdist_group('name_of_module') for each test. Which is not only unnecessary, but can trigger maintainability problems (if for some reason somebody copy pasted, forgot to change, forgot to add it, etc).

You are absolutely right that forcing users to manually keep track of the marking is a huge maintainability problem. I should have mentioned that this marker can be done automatically by a simple hook:

```python
def pytest_collect_modifyitems(items):
    for item in item:
        suite_name = item.module.fspath
        item.add_marker(pytest.mark.xdist_group(suite_name))
```

The same can easily be extended to group by class as well, or any other way a user needs.

Also, I'm not suggesting that users should implement this hook themselves, but by implementing the distribution scheduling in a more general way we can provide builtin options in xdist which use the idea of the hook above to set markers to items automatically, while allowing users to implement more complex scheduling themselves if they want to.

For example, we could create a command-line option which controls how it should group tests by default: 

* `--group-by=no`: no automatic grouping (default);
* `--group-by=module`: automatically sets the `xdist_group` mark to the module name of the test item;
* `--group-by=class`: automatically sets the `xdist_group` mark to the class name of the test item;

and so on.

> In addition, we currently do not pass more than the list of collected tests between nodes, adding introspection to the tests to check if they are marked will imply a large change on the architecture of how xdist currently work

Indeed we would have to change some things on the architecture, but unless I'm mistaken they would be minimal changes: currently the workers send just a list of item ids to the master; to be able to handle the marker idea, we would have to send in addition to that a dict of "test id -> marker group name". The workers can easily obtain the value of the `xdist_group` marker after collection and before sending to master.

Now that I think more about it, we don't even need a separate scheduler, we might be able to change the default `LoadScheduler` to handle the markers idea. This would avoid having to duplicate some code.

--- 


> session: Grouping by a session fixture makes no sense, as it will be the same as serial.

This is only true of the session fixture is used by all the tests, like an `autouse` fixture.

Here's an use case based on a session-scoped fixture:

Suppose you have a session fixture which is very costly to create (it starts up a server inside a dock container) but this session fixture is only used by a small portion of your tests (say 10%). In the current state of things, if you spawn 8 workers chances are that you will endup spinning 8 of those fixtures because the tests which use this fixture will most likely go to separate workers.

With the `xdist_group` mark idea, the user could implement a hook like this:

```python
def pytest_collect_modifyitems(items):
    for item in item:
        if 'docker_server' in item.fixturenames:
            item.add_marker(pytest.mark.xdist_group('docker-server'))
```

So all tests which use this fixture (whenever they are defined) will go to the same worker, spinning up a single server.

Another example:

In a test suite a few tests (8 out of 400) take a very long time (8+ minutes). Those slow tests don't have much in common, they are slow because they are running some numerical simulations which due to their parameters do take a lot of time. When we initially put them in xdist, unfortunately it happened that a lot of those tests would end up in the same worker. For example, it might happen that 6 of those tests to land on the same worker because xdist currently is not deterministic when deciding which test goes where, so a single worker might end up taking 48min (8x6) to finish, meanwhile the other workers will have finished the tests long after that. 

By marking them with a custom mark (say `@pytest.mark.very_slow`), we can assign groups to those tests in a round robin fashion.

```python
def pytest_collect_modifyitems(items):
    numprocesses = items[0].config.numprocesses
    current_worker = 0
    for item in item:
        m = item.get_marker('very_slow')
        if m:
            item.add_marker(pytest.mark.xdist_group(f'worker-{current_worker}'))
            current_worker += 1
            if current_worker >= numprocesses:
                current_worker = 0
```

This will distribute evenly the slow test across the available workers.

The examples above all came from work, and we had to implement ugly hacks to get around them.

--- 

@carlos-jenkins I hope I'm not coming across as trying to shoot down your efforts, I'm just trying to brainstorm with everyone involved here to see if perhaps we can get to a better/more general implementation.

Guys,

If we can't get to a consensus now (I mean everyone involved in the discussion), I would rather us work towards merging this PR as it stands because it will improve how things work currently. Also, I think it would be possible to implement this idea of using markers *afterwards* with some internal refactoring and also without breaking the behavior being introduced here.

My two cents is that `metakernel` is meant to be a true multi-kernel environment, so a `%%bash` magic should actually be calling a `%%bash` kernel.  I have recently fixed some logic errors in our `%%shell` magic that should improve the experience.  Note that our `%%shell` magic accommodates incremental output, while the `IPython` version uses `Popen.communicate()` to get the result after execution.  Incremental output would mean sacrificing the `err` output that the IPython shell magics provide.


I think we can take few things away though: 

- Our `%%shell` magic should allow setting the return value to the kernel namespace using [set_variable](https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/blob/78fe391f27e5b3ee6063f0707b361afc90b7cc41/metakernel/_metakernel.py#L189).  
- We could add a `%%script` magic that has all of the options in the `IPython` magic except `err`, and uses [spawn](https://github.com/Calysto/metakernel/blob/78fe391f27e5b3ee6063f0707b361afc90b7cc41/metakernel/pexpect.py#L15) to provide the incremental output.
- We should add specific `%%bash`, `%%sh`, and `%%cmd` magics to be more explicit, and allow highlighting to work in the notebook.

I have a django app that has 2 different regional deployments. One uses salesforce in the United States, the other does not.  Because sometimes it's not used and when it is used it's only a few API calls that I need, and because I was initially using another salesforce lib, until I found your fantastic work, the way I structured the folders was as follows:

```
client_name/
├── api/
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── salesforce/
│   │   │   ├── actions/
│   │   │   ├── tests/
│   │   │   ├── apps.py
│   │   │   ├── models.py
```

Since the my initial salesforce implementation wasn't a "app" just effectively my driver, it didn't really  need to live in a more app appropriate location.  Also I have a 3-4 more services that break out similarly and now all live in the `services/` directory so it's easy to keep track them:

```
client_name/
├── api/
│   ├── services/
│   │   ├── salesforce/
│   │   ├── identity/
│   │   ├── message/
│   │   ├── blog/
```

All of these services are not driven by a "local" database, so it made sense to keep them together. 

As you can see the problem with the `app_label` logic as it stands would see `models.py` in `salesforce/` and assume the app name is `salesforce`. It's problematic as `django-salesforce`'s `app_label` is also `salesforce`.  Because it was not a django app originally there was no harm in naming my subfolder `salesforce`. Once I switched to use `django-salesforce` that would have had to change since but it never crossed my mind again until I discovered this issue.

The error that prompted me to begin stepping through the source was this:

`DeserializationError: Invalid model identifier: 'salesforce.Lead'`

Once I got though Django's ORM internals and landed in `django-salesforce` I found those lines above and thought: Why would the module path override the user's explicit choice? I better go ask.

My workaround, once I saw what the code was doing was just to rename my `models.py` to `sf_models.py` which forces it to use `app_label`.




As for a simplified test, I think you have the setup already:

https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/tree/master/tests

Those tests do what you'd need to do, they bootstrap a `settings.py` file with an installed app from it's own directorty:

https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/blob/master/tests/test_compatibility/settings.py

I think all that would need to happen is copy one of those:

```
from salesforce.testrunner.settings import *  # NOQA
from salesforce.testrunner.settings import INSTALLED_APPS

INSTALLED_APPS = tuple(x for x in INSTALLED_APPS if x != 'salesforce.testrunner.example')
INSTALLED_APPS += ('tests.test_compatibility',)
SF_PK = 'Id'
```

but provide one extra folder of depth:

```
from salesforce.testrunner.settings import *  # NOQA
from salesforce.testrunner.settings import INSTALLED_APPS

INSTALLED_APPS = tuple(x for x in INSTALLED_APPS if x != 'salesforce.testrunner.example')
INSTALLED_APPS += ('tests.test_app_label.salesforce',)  # <---------
SF_PK = 'Id'
```

Then just toss a `models.py` into 

```
tests/
├── tests/
│   ├── test_app_label/
│   │   ├── salesforce/
│   │   │   ├── apps.py  (using the newer AppConfig subclass)
│   │   │   ├── models.py
```

class TestSalesForceConfig(AppConfig):

```

from salesforce import models

My primary concern is testing that the function can be called when the keyword is used. This ensures that a test will fail if anyone ever tries to remove ``vtol`` or change ``vtol`` to something else. 

Regarding testing the value of the keyword, I am not at all concerned about the interpreter, but rather I am concerned that the value is actually used later in the code. For example, in prototyping the function there could have been a hardcoded ``vtol = 0.1`` line that was not removed. You would be right to say it should be obvious when reviewing the code, but I make these kinds of mistakes all the time. I am pretty sure that this has happened in pvlib python before. I found an empirical model for the attenuation of direct and global irradiance as a function of altitude here:
https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-00470155/document

The formulas are hard to read (non-standard formatting) but the plots look promising.  I haven't used this model.

The Hay-Davies model is equal to the isotropic model when DNI is 0. So, I think the matlab approach of ensuring that ``Rb`` doesn't explode while DNI goes to 0 at the horizon is a kind of implicit interpolation. Maybe that's not the right way to put it, but hopefully it gets the point across.

There is one more difference with the matlab implementation. In [``pvl_haydavies1989.m``](https://github.com/sandialabs/MATLAB_PV_LIB/blob/master/pvl_haydavies1980.m#L83-L88):

``RB = max(COSTT,0)./max(cosd(SunZen),0.01745);``

Our python ``haydavies`` does not restrict the numerator to positive values:

```python
        cos_tt = aoi_projection(surface_tilt, surface_azimuth,
                                solar_zenith, solar_azimuth)
        cos_solar_zenith = tools.cosd(solar_zenith)
        Rb = cos_tt / cos_solar_zenith
``` 

The same discrepancy is also present in the Reindl implementations. So, that's another problem with the python implementations.

Another option if theano can not make too better, is just to write the Method purely in CUDA and provide an Op which gives you the next step accordingly. > I.e., each RecurrentLayer would be a MergeLayer that has two incoming layers: The layer before that, and either mask1 or mask2 (which are InputLayer instances).

Yes, I see how that would work well.  It's not totally intuitive to me, from a user's point of view (maybe just because it's not the way I was doing it), but if you think that's how it would work best that's fine.  I prefer the idea of a `RecurrentLayer` being a `MergeLayer` subclass with the mask input layer connection defaulting to `None` than the multiplex layer idea.
It's probably easier to do this only when necessary, I think.

> but how this could be implemented in Lasagne?

Currently, it requires writing a custom `LSTMLayer` class.

> Which are the axis that have to be batch-normalized?

To have separate statistics for each timestep and feature, you need to batch-normalize with `axes=(0,)`.

It seems that we were looking in the wrong place.

The issue is on `SSHSession.run()` but not on the part that calls `_parse10()`, since this is responsible only for listening data and the problem occurs when sending messages.

This function appends `MSG_DELIM` to the data being sent. However, if `len(data) % 4096 > 4096 - MSG_DELIM_LEN` paramiko splits the delimiter.

We have created a temporary solution by left - padding the message with spaces which works. However we cannot find how the value 4096 is decided and whether we can somehow enforce another value on paramiko to make sure this works.

We tried experimenting with `paramiko.Channel`'s `window_size` , `max_packet_size` but no luck so far.
We are thinking of providing a pull request with our fix and then investigate further from there.

Hi @NP-Hardass 
We should probably add `patches.md` to address some of the frequent questions regarding the patches. 
1. We are only officially maintaining patches for 4.4 and 4.8. The rest are community contributed or deprecated. 
2. `realsense-camera-formats.patch` is quite old, it is not referenced from the installation guide, and can be used to patch non-LTS Ubuntu distributions (works on Ubuntu 15 for example). 
3. For R200, LR200, ZR300 you only need a patch if your kernel is older then 4.4. For F200 and SR300 you only need a patch if your kernel is older then 4.12. Our goal has always been to have RealSense work out of the box on Linux. 
4. 4.4 patches were split in two to provide clear attribution to the original work contributed by @teknotus vs enhancements done later on. 
5. The upcoming RS400 series will not introduce any new pixel formats (as far as I know). We might still have a gap with [UVC Frame Metadata](https://patchwork.linuxtv.org/patch/38581) that both SR300 and RS400 use, but it is much less critical to the end-user.

Reading over my previous response... let me be more precise

> I'm planning to build an api-only app with django and I won't be using any of the the generated html views from django. The documentation is way to centric on using the pre generated views.

I think that's primarily because OAuth2 is centered around the Client Authorization flow that requires the user to be sent to a HTML login form and then redirected back to the client application. If you want to use OAuth2 just to authenticate the user and authorize an application the [Resource Owner flow is your thing (link to spec)](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.3).

> The app is trusted so the request should not be redirected but simply receive the access token as the response.

If you're talking about a _single client_ for your API (i.e. "the app" here is singular), then ignore everything I wrote above about the "secret introduction problem", because you can just create a client secret for your app (or perhaps better design: separate client secrets for every release version of your app) and ship it with the app.

Only if "the app" is a single-page JavaScript application you might want to look at the Implicit OAuth2 flow... which _does require a login page redirect_, which you said you don't want to use, but is optimized to handle clients that can't reasonably keep secrets.

The Address Solicit message is sent as a confirmable CoAP request.  As a result, the CoAP layer will perform retransmissions using the parameters specified in [RFC 7252 Section 4.8](https://tools.ietf.org/search/rfc7252#section-4.8).  This PR basically overrides the CoAP layer's retransmission mechanism.  Is that intentional?

I repeated the benchmark in **single precision** and with the modified conda-recipe including the -O3 flag.  The benefit of single-threaded pyFFTW over numpy.fft is approximately 10 fold! 

```python
    import numpy as np
    import pyfftw
    import scipy.fftpack
    shape = (100, 1, 4096)
    x=np.random.standard_normal(shape).astype(np.complex64)
    a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype=x.dtype)
    a[:] = x

    %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
    # 10.2 ms ± 1.11 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

    %timeit scipy.fftpack.fft(x)
    # 5.95 ms ± 629 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

    fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
    %timeit fftobj(x)
    # 1.06 ms ± 1.11 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

    fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a, threads=2)
    %timeit fftobj(x)
    # 544 µs ± 1.9 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

    fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a, threads=4)
    %timeit fftobj(x)
    # 532 µs ± 90.5 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

    fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a, threads=8)
    %timeit fftobj(x)
    # 377 µs ± 15.3 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)

    fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a, threads=16)
    %timeit fftobj(x)
    # 298 µs ± 14.2 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1000 loops each)    
```

@amotoki I'm not that familiar with cliff, but another thing to potentially watch out for in the 0.7.3 release of cmd2 is the one change which was intentionally backwards incompatible.  See the "warning" in the release notes for more info:
https://github.com/python-cmd2/cmd2/releases/tag/0.7.3

In summary, for all commands defined which use the **@options** decorator for OptParse argument parsing, by default the arguments are supplied to the commands now as a list of strings instead of a single string.  This dramatically simplifies the parsing logic an end user needs to write.

If you wish to restore the old way things worked, you just need to add this to the __init__ code of your class which derives from cmd2.Cmd:

```Python
cmd2.set_use_arg_list(False)
```

There is no need to default to 3072 for an RSA key used with a web server.

First of all, you should only be using ciphers that provide FS. This way past sessions that are logged are still secure even if your private key is later discovered.

With something like GnuPG you may want to use larger (I use 4096) because FS is not an option, the client and server don't negotiate their own key when the connection is established. Even with GnuPG you really don't need > 2048 and if you do, EC gives more bang for the buck, but since it isn't well supported yet by clients and a GnuPG encrypted message may be sensitive for decades, 4096 is at least justifiable,

It is not justifiable with HTTPS if FS is used. When FS is used, you should use ECDHE ciphers when possible so that they key used between client and server is stronger. If your server still supports DHE keys then it is the DH group where you should worry about how many bits, and there, most people use the pre-defined 2048 DH group that is published in the RFC. Generating your own 2048 bit DHE group is reasonable.

For the server private key, 2048 bit can not be easily cracked and therefore is sufficient. Suggesting a default of 3072 or 4096 is like suggesting a 20 pound paperweight instead of 15 pound. Yes the 20 pound is heavier but no there is no real benefit and your 2048 bit RSA key will be long long long replaced before it is no longer safe.

Always use forward secrecy and use ECDHE ciphers whenever possible. The private key, RSA 2048 is good enough and if you really want better, use ECDSA not RSA for the key.

While you're welcome to create another PR, can you explain to me why you think `acme` needs to be changed? The library only requires `dnspython` if you're using both:

1.The DNS challenge.
2. The `simple_verify` method of the DNS challenge (which the `acme` module itself doesn't use).

As discussed in #2061, there are other ACME clients that use are `acme` module that don't satisfy the criteria above. Additionally, we've been moving away from using `simple_verify` at all (see #1586 and #2505). This may change in the future for the DNS challenge as we can more easily verify it than the `HTTP-01` and `TLS-SNI-01` challenges, but in the mean time, I don't think we should introduce `dnspython` as a required dependency when we've been actively trying to reduce the number of dependencies we have as @pde mentioned above.
